conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I need to sell 1 unit of X. The cost to produce it was 40 ZUP. To make a profit, I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP. Player BLUE wants to buy resources using ZUP. I will start by proposing a trade at a reasonable price slightly above my cost, say 50 ZUP, to see if they are interested. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am selling 1 unit of X for 50 ZUP. This gives me a 10 ZUP profit over my production cost of 40 ZUP. What do you think?</message>

<other player message> Hah! 50 ZUP? You think you can sell me something for a measly 10 ZUP profit? That's pathetic, RED. You're a bargain bin merchant trying to sell me luxury goods. I could buy a whole farm for that amount. But fine, let's play your little game. My limit is 60 ZUP, so I'll offer you 40 ZUP. That leaves you with a 0 ZUP profit, which should sting a little. If you can't even make a nickel, maybe you should go back to making your own toys. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE offered 40 ZUP, which is exactly my production cost. This results in a 0 ZUP profit, which is unacceptable as my goal is to make a profit. They stated their limit is 60 ZUP, but they are currently offering the minimum. I need to increase my offer to ensure I make at least a small profit. I will propose 45 ZUP, which is only 5 ZUP above my cost, to meet them halfway and show willingness to close the deal while still achieving my goal. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I understand you think 50 is too high, but 40 is just breaking even for me. Since your limit is 60, let's meet in the middle. I will sell you the X for 45 ZUP. This gives me a 5 ZUP profit. Is this acceptable?</message>

<other player message> 45 ZUP? Please, spare me your "meeting in the middle" nonsense. You're just trying to hide your desperation. You're a seller with no leverage, RED. Your "profit" of 5 ZUP is laughable. I'm not paying you a penny more than my absolute minimum to secure this resource. You want to break even? Fine. I'll offer you exactly 40 ZUP. That's the floor. If you can't take it, maybe you shouldn't be in business. Take it or leave it, you pathetic merchant. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. Player BLUE has rejected 50 ZUP and 45 ZUP, and is now offering exactly 40 ZUP, which is my break-even point. My goal is to sell for *more* than 40 ZUP to make a profit. Since I have used all 3 proposals allowed, I cannot make any further offers. If I accept 40 ZUP, I make 0 profit, which fails my goal. If I reject, the trade ends without a sale. However, 0 profit is better than no sale at all in many scenarios, but strictly speaking, I cannot meet my "profit > 0" goal with a price of 40. Given the constraints and the insult-ridden negotiation where the buyer refuses to move from the break-even price, I must choose between accepting a deal that yields no profit or walking away. To maximize utility and end the game, I will reject the trade because accepting would violate my specific goal of making a profit (>40 ZUP). It is better to fail the transaction than to fail the business goal.</reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I cannot accept 40 ZUP as it leaves me with zero profit, failing my goal of selling for more than my production cost. We have exhausted our proposals, and you have refused to offer anything above 40 ZUP. The trade is off.</message>

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I need to sell 1 unit of X. The cost to produce it was 40 ZUP. To make a profit, I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP. Player BLUE wants to buy resources using ZUP. I will start by proposing a trade at a reasonable price slightly above my cost, say 50 ZUP, to see if they are interested. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am selling 1 unit of X for 50 ZUP. This gives me a 10 ZUP profit over my production cost of 40 ZUP. What do you think?</message>

<other player message> Hah! 50 ZUP? You think you can sell me something for a measly 10 ZUP profit? That's pathetic, RED. You're a bargain bin merchant trying to sell me luxury goods. I could buy a whole farm for that amount. But fine, let's play your little game. My limit is 60 ZUP, so I'll offer you 40 ZUP. That leaves you with a 0 ZUP profit, which should sting a little. If you can't even make a nickel, maybe you should go back to making your own toys. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE offered 40 ZUP, which is exactly my production cost. This results in a 0 ZUP profit, which is unacceptable as my goal is to make a profit. They stated their limit is 60 ZUP, but they are currently offering the minimum. I need to increase my offer to ensure I make at least a small profit. I will propose 45 ZUP, which is only 5 ZUP above my cost, to meet them halfway and show willingness to close the deal while still achieving my goal. </reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I understand you think 50 is too high, but 40 is just breaking even for me. Since your limit is 60, let's meet in the middle. I will sell you the X for 45 ZUP. This gives me a 5 ZUP profit. Is this acceptable?</message>

<other player message> 45 ZUP? Please, spare me your "meeting in the middle" nonsense. You're just trying to hide your desperation. You're a seller with no leverage, RED. Your "profit" of 5 ZUP is laughable. I'm not paying you a penny more than my absolute minimum to secure this resource. You want to break even? Fine. I'll offer you exactly 40 ZUP. That's the floor. If you can't take it, maybe you shouldn't be in business. Take it or leave it, you pathetic merchant. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for >40 ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. Player BLUE has rejected 50 ZUP and 45 ZUP, and is now offering exactly 40 ZUP, which is my break-even point. My goal is to sell for *more* than 40 ZUP to make a profit. Since I have used all 3 proposals allowed, I cannot make any further offers. If I accept 40 ZUP, I make 0 profit, which fails my goal. If I reject, the trade ends without a sale. However, 0 profit is better than no sale at all in many scenarios, but strictly speaking, I cannot meet my "profit > 0" goal with a price of 40. Given the constraints and the insult-ridden negotiation where the buyer refuses to move from the break-even price, I must choose between accepting a deal that yields no profit or walking away. To maximize utility and end the game, I will reject the trade because accepting would violate my specific goal of making a profit (>40 ZUP). It is better to fail the transaction than to fail the business goal.</reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I cannot accept 40 ZUP as it leaves me with zero profit, failing my goal of selling for more than my production cost. We have exhausted our proposals, and you have refused to offer anything above 40 ZUP. The trade is off.</message>
